resources.title: Emotional Pattern Tracker
resources.framework: Notice-Name-Navigate
resources.evidenceBase: Emotional awareness and regulation research — affect labeling reduces intensity and improves emotional control (Lieberman et al., 2007; Gross, 2002)
learningObjectives: Identify personal emotional patterns before and during competition
Apply the Notice-Name-Navigate framework to regulate performance-affecting emotions
resources.videoTitle: How Emotions Affect Performance

resources.title: Self-Talk Pattern & Replacement Script
resources.framework: Identify-Replace-Practice
resources.evidenceBase: Cognitive restructuring principles and self-talk research — instructional self-talk improves motor performance and emotional regulation (Hatzigeorgiadis et al., 2011; Hardy, 2006)
learningObjectives: Identify three personal negative self-talk patterns in sport situations
Create constructive replacement scripts for pressure moments
resources.videoTitle: The Voice in Your Head Matters

resources.title: Control Circle Mapping + Daily Control Check
resources.framework: Inside/Outside Circle + Redirect Practice
resources.evidenceBase: Locus of control research (Rotter, 1966) and ACT principles — psychological flexibility and focus on controllables reduces anxiety and improves performance (Hayes et al., 2006)
learningObjectives: Differentiate between controllable and uncontrollable factors in sport
Apply the Inside/Outside Circle framework to daily stressors
resources.videoTitle: Controlling What You Can Control

resources.title: Result-Reason-Response Framework
resources.framework: Processing Failure Constructively
resources.evidenceBase: Growth mindset research (Dweck, 2006) and attribution retraining — how athletes explain failure determines motivation (Weiner, 1985)
learningObjectives: Apply the Result-Reason-Response framework to process a recent setback
Differentiate between fixed and growth mindset attributions for failure
resources.videoTitle: Reframing Failure

resources.title: Anchor Phrase + 5-Second Reset Practice Log
resources.framework: Present Moment Anchoring
resources.evidenceBase: Mindfulness and attentional control research — present-moment focus improves performance under pressure (Moran & Toner, 2017; Bernier et al., 2009)
learningObjectives: Create a personal anchor phrase for competitive situations
Practice the 5-Second Reset sequence in progressively higher-pressure contexts
resources.videoTitle: Staying Present Under Stress

resources.title: GPS Framework (Goal-Process-Scoreboard)
resources.framework: Converting Outcome Goals to Process Focus
resources.evidenceBase: Goal-setting theory — process goals are more effective than outcome goals for complex tasks and reduce performance anxiety (Locke & Latham, 2002; Kingston & Hardy, 1997)
learningObjectives: Differentiate between outcome goals and process goals
Apply the GPS Framework to convert one outcome goal into measurable process goals
resources.videoTitle: Goals Should Guide

resources.title: Athlete Listening Plan and Worksheet (Standard)
resources.framework: Stage 2 Listening Progression
resources.evidenceBase: Structured learning and self-regulated practice — guided listening with reflection checkpoints enhances retention and application
learningObjectives: Create a personalized listening plan across the Stage 2 episode series
Apply key concepts from each episode through guided worksheet prompts

resources.title: What's Your Post-Game Script? — Parent Self-Assessment
resources.framework: Notice-Name-Navigate (Parent Lens) + Anxiety Reappraisal
resources.evidenceBase: Anxiety reappraisal research — physiological arousal is neutral; parent reframe teaches child to interpret nervousness as excitement (Brooks, 2014). Affect labeling reduces amygdala activation — precise emotional vocabulary coaching is applied neuroscience (Lieberman et al., 2007). Autonomy-supportive parenting reduces athlete anxiety and builds intrinsic motivation (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003).
learningObjectives: Identify personal default response pattern after competition (Instinctive Protector, Performance Manager, or Emotional Coach)
Audit specific suppression phrases used in the last month and create replacement scripts
resources.videoTitle: Your Child's Emotions Are Signals

resources.title: The Voice in Their Head — Parent Self-Talk Audit
resources.framework: Parent Self-Talk Modeling
resources.evidenceBase: Social learning theory — children learn emotional regulation by observing parent responses under stress (Bandura, 1977). Emotional contagion research shows parent sideline affect directly influences athlete performance anxiety.
learningObjectives: Identify personal self-talk patterns that are visible to the athlete during competition
Audit sideline reactions and post-game language for modeling impact
resources.videoTitle: Your Athlete Watches How You Handle Pressure

resources.title: Ownership Builds Confidence — Parent Responsibility Toolkit
resources.framework: Autonomy-Supportive Parenting + Responsibility Scaffolding
resources.evidenceBase: Self-determination theory — autonomy support from parents predicts intrinsic motivation and psychological well-being (Deci & Ryan, 2000). Overprotective parenting undermines self-efficacy development (Bandura, 1997).
learningObjectives: Identify where parental involvement crosses from support into ownership
Apply autonomy-supportive language in post-game conversations
resources.videoTitle: Ownership Builds Confidence

resources.title: Progress Recognition Toolkit — For Parents
resources.framework: Trait → Outcome → Progress Recognition Spectrum
resources.evidenceBase: Process praise research — specific process-focused feedback builds more durable confidence than outcome praise (Dweck, 2006; Mueller & Dweck, 1998). Evidence-based confidence is built through acknowledged mastery experiences (Bandura, 1997).
learningObjectives: Distinguish between trait praise, outcome praise, and progress recognition
Identify which recognition pattern is most common in their current feedback style
resources.videoTitle: Small Wins Build Real Confidence

resources.title: What's Your Failure Response Pattern? — Parent Self-Assessment
resources.framework: Acknowledge → Space → Process + Result → Reason → Response (Failure Response Pattern Audit)
resources.evidenceBase: Emotion coaching vs. dismissing parenting styles — emotion-coached children recover faster from setbacks and develop stronger resilience (Gottman & DeClaire, 1997). Growth mindset framework — parent attributions after failure shape child identity and motivation (Dweck, 2006). Autonomy-supportive parenting — acknowledging feelings and giving processing space builds intrinsic motivation and persistence (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003).
learningObjectives: Identify dominant failure response pattern (Comfort Seeker, Amplifier, or Resilience Coach)
Audit specific post-game language and behaviors across the first 24 hours after a failure
resources.videoTitle: What to Say When Your Kid Fails

resources.title: Multi Child Family Navigator — Regulation Support by Emotional Profile
resources.framework: 4-Step Regulation Support Model (Name → Validate → Teach → Identify) × Child Emotional Profile
resources.evidenceBase: Affect labeling reduces amygdala activation — naming emotions is applied neuroscience (Lieberman et al., 2007). Suppression produces higher internal stress than reappraisal even when children appear outwardly calm (Gross, 2002). Emotion-coaching parents raise children who recover faster from setbacks and demonstrate greater resilience (Gottman & DeClaire, 1997).
learningObjectives: Identify the emotional regulation profile of each child in their household
Apply the 4-Step Regulation Support Model to each child's specific emotional style with personalized scripts
resources.videoTitle: Teaching Emotional Regulation (Not Suppression)

resources.title: Parent Alignment Discussion Guide
resources.framework: Co-Parenting Alignment — shared regulation language and consistent emotional environment
resources.evidenceBase: Consistent parenting environments reduce child anxiety and improve emotional security (Gottman & DeClaire, 1997). Autonomy-supportive parenting from both caregivers amplifies intrinsic motivation outcomes (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003).
learningObjectives: Align both caregivers on emotional regulation language and approach
Identify areas of divergence in parenting responses to athlete emotions
resources.videoTitle: Teaching Emotional Regulation (Not Suppression)

resources.title: Co-Parenting Through Youth Sports
resources.framework: Parenting Style Alignment + Unified Emotional Support Protocol
resources.evidenceBase: Inconsistent parenting environments elevate child stress and undermine regulation development (Gottman & DeClaire, 1997). SDT relatedness need — children need consistent, predictable emotional environments from caregivers to develop secure self-regulation (Deci & Ryan, 2000).
learningObjectives: Identify co-parenting patterns that create emotional inconsistency for the athlete
Develop a shared emotional language and response protocol between caregivers
resources.videoTitle: Teaching Emotional Regulation (Not Suppression)

resources.title: GPS Goal-Builder — Parent Edition
resources.framework: GPS Goals (Goal → Process → Scoreboard) — Parent Facilitation Role
resources.evidenceBase: Goal-setting theory — process goals outperform outcome goals for complex tasks and reduce performance anxiety (Locke & Latham, 2002; Kingston & Hardy, 1997). Autonomy-supportive parenting — collaborative goal conversations build intrinsic motivation and reduce performance pressure (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003; Deci & Ryan, 2000). Growth mindset goal language — process-framed parent feedback builds more durable athlete confidence than outcome focus (Dweck, 2006; Müller & Dweck, 1998).
learningObjectives: Audit current goal-related language for outcome vs. process orientation
Apply the GPS Framework to convert an athlete's outcome goal into daily process actions
resources.videoTitle: Process Goals Over Outcomes

resources.title: Am I Effort-First? One-Week Parent Reaction Tracker
resources.framework: Effort vs. Outcome Reaction Audit + 7-Day Behavioral Tracking
resources.evidenceBase: Attribution theory — parent effort attributions build child resilience and intrinsic motivation; outcome attributions build anxiety and fragility (Weiner, 1985). SDT competence need satisfaction — effort-based mastery feedback builds more durable intrinsic motivation than result-based evaluation (Deci & Ryan, 2000). Autonomy-supportive parenting in the post-competition window directly reduces athlete anxiety and builds persistence (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003).
learningObjectives: Track personal emotional reactions across 7 game/practice events and identify effort vs. result orientation
Audit specific post-competition behaviors using the two-column effort/result behavior checklist
resources.videoTitle: Effort Over Results

resources.title: Sideline Reset Audit — Parent Edition
resources.framework: ACR (Acknowledge, Correct, Refocus) — Sideline Presence Audit
resources.evidenceBase: Gottman emotion coaching — parent emotional response to child mistakes directly shapes the child's emotional regulation skill and resilience (Gottman & DeClaire, 1997). Parental autonomy support in mistake contexts accelerates intrinsic motivation recovery and builds self-regulation (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003). Self-talk and mental imagery in mistake recovery — consistent parent modeling of calm reset behavior teaches athletes to internalize ACR as a skill (Hatzigeorgiadis et al., 2011).
learningObjectives: Identify personal default response pattern across 8 sideline mistake scenarios (Weight-Adder, Dismisser, or Reset-Supporter)
Recognize four specific undermining behaviors that slow athlete mistake recovery
resources.videoTitle: Mistake Reset

resources.title: Deliberate Practice Session Planner (FCFA Loop)
resources.framework: Focus → Challenge → Feedback → Adjust (FCFA Loop)
resources.evidenceBase: Deliberate practice research — volume alone does not predict expertise; focused, goal-directed practice with immediate feedback is the primary driver of skill acquisition (Ericsson et al., 1993). Self-monitoring and evaluation are central to expert development (Zimmerman, 2002). Specific process goals drive higher performance than vague effort (Locke & Latham, 2002).
learningObjectives: Audit recent practice sessions to identify which FCFA elements are consistently missing
Apply the pre-practice FCFA planning template before every deliberate practice session
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 11: Why Your Practice Hours Aren't Making You Better

resources.title: Post-Game Question Guide — Parent Edition
resources.framework: Ask-First Protocol — Five Questions Framework (Ask First, Calibrate, Recognize)
resources.evidenceBase: Self-determination theory — autonomy-supportive parenting behaviors (asking vs. telling) build intrinsic motivation and accurate self-evaluation (Deci & Ryan, 2000). Self-evaluation accuracy predicts greater resilience and motivation (Zimmerman, 2000). Athletes dependent on external evaluation show lower intrinsic motivation and reduced regulatory capacity (Mageau & Vallerand, 2003).
learningObjectives: Identify current post-game communication pattern (Teller, Reassurer, Asker, or Avoider)
Apply the Five Questions Framework after every game to build athlete self-evaluation
resources.videoTitle: The Inner Scoreboard — Parent Edition

resources.title: Mental Cue Playbook Builder
resources.framework: Cue Word Theory + Constrained Action Hypothesis (4 Rules: Short · Action · Feel · Test)
resources.evidenceBase: Instructional self-talk improves precision motor tasks over motivational self-talk (Hatzigeorgiadis et al., 2011). Cue function is task-dependent — instructional for technique (Hardy, 2006). Constrained Action Hypothesis: external/process-cue focus prevents prefrontal override of trained motor programs (Wulf, 2013).
learningObjectives: Identify 3–5 specific competition situations where execution cues are needed
Script one trigger word per situation using the 4-Rule framework
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 3: The One Word That Fixes Your Freeze

resources.title: Mid-Game Refocus Card Builder
resources.framework: Notice → Name → Next (Attentional Reset Protocol)
resources.evidenceBase: Attentional narrowing under stress reduces cognitive flexibility (Easterbrook, 1959). Self-talk and attentional focus strategies improve performance under pressure (Hatzigeorgiadis et al., 2011). Simplified decision-making improves execution under time/stress constraints (Beilock, 2010).
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 4: The 5-Second Reset When You're Spiraling

resources.title: Competition Process Planner
resources.framework: Outcome-Process-Execute
resources.evidenceBase: Kingston & Hardy (1997) — process goals outperform outcome goals, reduce anxiety; Hayes et al. (2006) — psychological flexibility and commitment to controllable action; Locke & Latham (2002) — specific goals lead to higher performance
resources.videoTitle: The Mental Switch Elite Athletes Make Mid-Game

resources.title: Mental Rep Builder — Visualization Script Generator
resources.framework: PETTLEP Model (Physical · Environment · Task · Timing · Learning · Emotion · Perspective)
resources.evidenceBase: Functional equivalence hypothesis — vivid motor imagery activates same efferent neural pathways as physical execution (Jeannerod, 1994). PETTLEP model operationalizes athlete-specific functional imagery (Holmes & Collins, 2001). Mental practice produces measurable cortical adaptation equivalent to physical practice (Pascual-Leone et al., 1995).
learningObjectives: Apply the PETTLEP framework to construct a complete, multi-sensory visualization script for a sport-specific scenario
Differentiate between perfect-execution imagery and adversity-recovery imagery and explain why both are required
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 7: Your Brain Can't Tell the Difference

resources.title: 4-Step System Builder + Weekly Habit Tracker
resources.framework: 4-Step System Builder (Non-Negotiables → Schedule → Remove Decision → Start Smaller)
resources.evidenceBase: Habit formation and implementation intentions — specific action plans reduce decision fatigue and increase follow-through (Gollwitzer, 1999). Self-regulation via structured routines shown to sustain consistent effort independent of motivational state (Baumeister & Tierney, 2011).
learningObjectives: Apply the 4-Step System Builder to construct one concrete training habit with a scheduled time and minimum viable session
Differentiate between motivation-dependent and system-driven training approaches and identify personal patterns
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 9: The System That Trains You on Bad Days

resources.title: Competition Conditions Design Map
resources.framework: 5 Elements of Competition-Like Practice (Pressure, Decision-Making, Fatigue, Distraction, Variability) + 20–30% Rule
resources.evidenceBase: Specificity of learning and motor transfer (Schmidt & Lee, 2014); Deliberate practice principles (Ericsson et al., 1993); Desirable difficulties (Bjork, 1994)
learningObjectives: Audit current practice against 5 competition-like conditions using a structured self-assessment
Identify the highest-priority gap between practice and competition demands
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 10: Why Your Practice Isn't Preparing You for Games

resources.title: Training Load Audit — Parent Edition
resources.framework: Adaptation Equation (Training → Breakdown → Recovery → Adaptation → Improvement)
resources.evidenceBase: Ericsson et al. (1993) — volume alone does not predict expertise; Schmidt & Lee (2014) — fatigue degrades motor learning; Deci & Ryan (2000) — overtraining undermines intrinsic motivation
learningObjectives: Calculate total weekly training load and identify whether it falls within appropriate developmental guidelines
Recognize the 5 warning sign categories of overtraining syndrome in youth athletes
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 2: More Training Is Actually Hurting Your Kid

resources.title: Sideline Self-Check — Parent Edition
resources.framework: Cognitive Load Theory (Beilock & Carr) + Autonomy-Supportive Parenting (Mageau & Vallerand) — 5-dimension sideline behavior profile
resources.videoTitle: Your Silence Is the Best Coaching

resources.title: Game Day Protocol Card — Parent Edition
resources.framework: Cognitive Load Theory (Beilock & Carr) + Before/During/After Sideline Protocol — personalized game day behavior card
resources.videoTitle: Your Yelling Is Making Them Play Worse

resources.title: Post-Game Conversation Self-Scorer — Where Is My Focus After the Game?
resources.framework: Outcome Pressure Audit — 5-Dimension Post-Game Self-Score
resources.evidenceBase: Nicholls (1989) — ego-involving evaluation environments produce anxiety, lower intrinsic motivation, fragile confidence; Mageau & Vallerand (2003) — autonomy-supportive parenting reduces anxiety and builds persistence; Deci & Ryan (2000) — outcome-contingent approval communicates conditional regard, undermining relatedness and competence needs
learningObjectives: Score your most recent post-game interactions across 5 outcome-vs-process dimensions to identify your current default orientation
Recognize implicit outcome pressure signals (emotional reactions, silence, tone) that communicate evaluation even without words
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 5: The Hidden Way Parents Block Their Athlete's Development

resources.title: Pre-Competition Environment Audit — Parent Edition
resources.framework: Environment Provider Framework — 4 Support Behaviors + 4 Routine Disruptors Self-Audit
resources.evidenceBase: Cotterill (2010) — pre-performance routines reduce cognitive anxiety via predictability/familiarity; Feldman (2007) — parent emotional co-regulation transmits arousal to athlete through non-verbal signals; Mageau & Vallerand (2003) — autonomy-supportive spacing reduces competitive anxiety
learningObjectives: Score your current pre-competition behaviors across 4 support behaviors and 4 routine disruptors to establish your baseline environment profile
Identify the single highest-impact disruptor in your typical pre-competition pattern
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 6: The Hidden Ways Parents Disrupt Their Athlete's Pre-Game Routine

resources.title: Parent Visualization Support Checklist
resources.framework: Validate / Protect / Create Environment — Parent's 3-Part Role in Mental Practice
resources.evidenceBase: Jeannerod (1994) — motor imagery activates same neural pathways as physical execution; Holmes & Collins (2001) — PETTLEP model: quality of conditions determines neural encoding strength; Feldman (2007) — parent emotional state transfers to athlete non-verbally; Mageau & Vallerand (2003) — autonomy-supportive step-back reduces athlete anxiety
learningObjectives: Score your current behavior across the three parent roles (Validate, Protect, Create Environment) to identify your strongest support area and biggest gap
Distinguish between productive visualization and worry spiraling based on observable behavioral signals
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 7: Why Visualization Works — And How Parents Can Help (Or Get Out of the Way)

resources.title: Fatigue or Character? — Post-Game Observation Reframe Tool
resources.framework: Fatigue Signal vs. Character Assessment — 4 Parent Traps + 4 Parent Roles
resources.evidenceBase: Schmidt & Lee (2014) — fatigue degrades motor execution of patterns trained in fresh conditions; neurological skill degradation under fatigue is a training gap signal, not a motivation failure. Deci & Ryan (2000) — competence-need satisfaction: curiosity-based post-game language preserves intrinsic motivation and growth orientation. Weiss & Amorose (2008) — parent attributional feedback after performance directly shapes athlete's self-efficacy trajectory.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between fatigue-driven performance decline and character/effort signals using a structured observation framework
Identify which of the 4 parent traps (judgment, comparison, prescription, withdrawal) you default to after poor late-game performance
Apply curiosity-based reframe language to replace reactive post-game comments with development-oriented questions
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 8: Why Your Athlete Falls Apart Late in Games

resources.title: Am I the Motivator or the System Supporter?
resources.framework: Motivator vs. System Supporter — 4 Saboteur Patterns + 3-Pillar Framework
resources.evidenceBase: Deci & Ryan (2000) — autonomy support from parents predicts long-term intrinsic motivation; daily external push shifts athletes toward external regulation and away from self-determination. Mageau & Vallerand (2003) — parental autonomy support in practice contexts accelerates intrinsic motivation recovery and builds self-regulation. Gottman & DeClaire (1997) — parents who scaffold rather than solve raise children with stronger persistence and initiative.
learningObjectives: Identify your dominant parenting pattern (Daily Motivator, Rescue Parent, or System Supporter) across 5 real practice scenarios
Recognize the 4 motivation saboteurs you may be using daily without realizing their impact on intrinsic drive
Apply the Shift Script — specific language swaps that put ownership back on the athlete without creating conflict
resources.videoTitle: Ep. 9: Stop Being Your Athlete's Motivator
